hints.grano: 'Grano' is a noun meaning 'grain' in the dative/ablative

hints.to eat: 'To eat' is 'edere' or 'esse' (different verb) — 'est' here is from 'esse' (to be)

hints.perfect indicative: Perfect of 'esse' would be 'fuit' — 'est' is present
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sit' — 'est' is indicative

hints.edo, esse, edi, esum: These are the principal parts of 'edere' (to eat) — the infinitive looks identical but the verb is different
hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the principal parts of 'ire' (to go)
hints.fio, fieri, factus sum, —: These are the principal parts of 'fieri' (to become/be made)

hints.accusative: 'Regnum' can be accusative, but here it is the subject of 'est' — nominative
hints.vocative: Vocative of a neuter noun is the same as nominative, but 'kingdom' is not being addressed

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'regni' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'regni' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'regni' ends in -i

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'Deus' — 'Dei' is the genitive singular
hints.dative: Dative would be 'Deo' — 'Dei' ends in -i marking genitive

hints.ablative: 'Grano' could be ablative, but with 'simile' the dative of comparison is standard

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'grani' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'grani' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'grani' ends in -i

hints.dative: Dative would not fit; 'sinapis' modifies 'grano' as 'grain of mustard'
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'sinapi' — 'sinapis' is the genitive form

translation: The kingdom of God is like a grain of mustard
